text.title: Technical Debt Classification and Scoring
text.summary: Six debt types, the prudent/reckless quadrant, scoring formula for prioritization, and capacity allocation approaches.

list.item: Deliberate — conscious shortcut: "Ship now, refactor later"
Accidental — learned better way after the fact: "Didn't know about X pattern"
Bit-rot — environment changed: dependencies outdated, platform APIs deprecated
Design — architecture limitations: "Can't scale current approach"
Documentation — missing docs: "No one knows how this works"
Test — missing tests: "Can't safely change X without coverage"

rule.statement: Distinguish Prudent Deliberate debt (conscious shortcut with plan to fix) from Reckless debt (no design awareness). Only prudent debt is acceptable.
rule.rationale: Reckless debt — "no time for design" or "what's a design?" — compounds without a paydown trigger. Prudent deliberate debt has a known cost and a plan.

rule.statement: Test debt is not optional: untested code cannot be safely refactored. Prioritize test debt before structural refactoring.
rule.rationale: Refactoring untested code produces regressions that are invisible until production. Tests are the safety net that makes all other paydown possible.

rule.statement: Score each debt item on four factors (1-5 each): Interest (how much it slows velocity), Contagion (does it spread?), Effort (cost to fix), Alignment (proximity to planned work). Priority = (Interest x Alignment) / Effort.
rule.rationale: Interest × Alignment / Effort identifies debt that is hurting us now AND can be fixed cheaply because we're already touching that code. This is the lowest marginal-cost paydown strategy.

antipattern.description: Agent scoring style/lint issues as "design debt".
antipattern.reason: Style and formatting are cosmetic, not structural. Inflating the register with cosmetic items buries real debt. Apply a severity rubric and reject purely stylistic signals at the classification stage.

antipattern.description: Using LLM-generated time estimates ("2 days") without team calibration.
antipattern.reason: LLM effort estimates for debt paydown are confabulation; actual work is 5-10x more. Calibrate from past resolved-debt items in the register.

section.title: Capacity Allocation

rule.statement: Allocate 15-20% of sprint capacity continuously to debt paydown rather than saving it for "debt sprints".
rule.rationale: Debt sprints allow debt to re-accumulate between them. Sustained 15-20% allocation keeps the interest rate stable and builds the habit.

list.item: Boy Scout — clean as you touch, 30-min cap per PR to prevent scope creep
Debt sprints — quarterly focused sprint, only for large architectural work
Spike — specific investigation of an unknown, before adding to register

section.reference: Per-item register: type, location, impact (time tax + risk), fix effort, priority.
Quarterly scoring table with sprint plan and deferred list.
